patterns.title: True/False Asymptotic Claims
patterns.oneLiner: Compare growth rates using the formal definitions. Know the standard traps.
triggers: 'State true or false. No justification needed.'
A claim involving $f(n), g(n)$ and $O, \Omega, \Theta$
A claim about Merge Sort / Quick Sort runtime behavior
A claim about median-of-medians dominance ratio (e.g. '$13n/18$ can be greater than $M$')
patterns.technique: Go back to definition. Ask: can I find $(C, n_0)$? If not, is there a witness $n$ that breaks every attempt at $C$?
template: Know the hierarchy: constants $\prec$ logs $\prec$ polynomials $\prec$ exponentials.
Know log rules: $\log(xy) = \log x + \log y$, $\log(x^k) = k\log x$, base change.
Recognize classic recurrences by sight (MergeSort, BinarySearch, Karatsuba, Select).
For median-of-medians with group size $k$: guaranteed $\lceil k/2\rceil$ elements $\le M$ per qualifying group, times $n/(2k)$ groups.
pitfalls: $\Theta$ does NOT distribute through multiplication when one factor is only $O$-bounded.
$\sqrt{\log n} \ne \Theta(\log\sqrt n)$ — the RHS is $\tfrac{1}{2}\log n = \Theta(\log n)$.
Merge Sort is $\Theta(n\log n)$ in the WORST case. Quick Sort is the one with bad worst case.
$f = O(g)$ does NOT imply $2^f = O(2^g)$.
$f = O(g)$ and $h = O(g)$ does NOT imply $f = O(h)$.

examples.why: Six T/F claims covering exactly these traps: Theta-product, log-square-root, recurrence shape, Merge Sort vs Quick Sort, median-of-medians group-of-9.

patterns.title: Recursion Tree for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$
patterns.oneLiner: Draw the tree, count per-level work, sum.
triggers: 'Draw the recursion tree for this recurrence.'
'Calculate T(n) up to big-$\Theta$.'
Any recurrence of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ you haven't seen before
patterns.technique: At level $i$: $a^i$ nodes of size $n/b^i$, level work $a^i f(n/b^i)$. Height $\log_b n$. Leaves $n^{\log_b a}$. Identify regime (decreasing / constant / increasing geometric), sum accordingly.
template: Count per-level: $a^i$ nodes, each of size $n/b^i$, each doing $f(n/b^i)$ non-recursive work.
Level work = $a^i \cdot f(n/b^i)$.
Determine if the level-work is decreasing, constant, or increasing in $i$.
Decreasing → dominated by root, $\Theta(f(n))$. Upper-bound with infinite geometric $\sum r^i = 1/(1-r)$.
Constant per level → $\Theta(f(n) \log n)$.
Increasing → dominated by leaves, $\Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
Compute leaves separately: $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$ leaves, each cost = base-case cost.
Conclude with $\Theta$-bound.
pitfalls: Forgetting to include the leaf contribution (usually negligible if decreasing, but always check).
Using finite geometric where infinite is cleaner (both valid; infinite gives a clean constant upper bound).
Mis-reading height as $\log_a n$ instead of $\log_b n$.

examples.why: $T(n) = 3T(n/5) + n$: $a=3, b=5$, level work $n(3/5)^i$ — decreasing geometric → $\Theta(n)$.
$T(n) = 2T(n/3) + n$: $a=2, b=3$, level work $n(2/3)^i$ — same shape, $\Theta(n)$.
$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$: level work $2^i$, increasing. Dominated by leaves $n$. $\Theta(n)$.

patterns.title: Formal Induction for $T(n) = O(f(n))$
patterns.oneLiner: Prove a concrete inequality by strong induction. Must derive EXACT bound.
triggers: 'Provide a formal proof by induction.'
'Prove that $T(n) = O(f(n))$.'
After you've guessed a bound via recursion tree or substitution
patterns.technique: Strong induction: claim $T(n) \le Cf(n)$ for specific $C$. Base, I.H., plug into recurrence, arrive at EXACT $T(n) \le Cf(n)$. If stuck, strengthen I.H. with a subtracted lower-order term.
template: **Claim:** $\exists\, n_0, C$ s.t. $T(n) \le Cf(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.
**Base case:** check $T(n_0)$; note what $C$ needs.
**I.H.:** for all $n_0 \le k < n$, $T(k) \le Cf(k)$. (STRONG induction, CONCRETE inequality — never asymptotic.)
**Step:** substitute I.H. into recurrence. Manipulate to exactly $T(n) \le Cf(n)$ — no extra $+1$, no $(C+1)f(n)$.
**Pick $C$:** satisfy both base and step.
pitfalls: Writing I.H. as $T(k) = O(k^2)$ — meaningless! Always concrete.
Ending inductive step with $T(n) \le (C+1)f(n)$ and thinking it's done. It's not; those constants compound.
Forgetting that $C$ must be the SAME throughout the proof.
If straightforward I.H. leaves leftover terms, strengthen: try $T(k) \le Ck^2 - dk$ instead.

examples.why: $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/3) + n$: claim $T(n) \le 6n$. Step: $T(n) \le 6n/2 + 6n/3 + n = 6n$. Exact.
$T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$: plain $T(k) \le Ck^2$ fails. Strengthen to $Ck^2 - k$.
Guess $T(n) = n + c$; solve $c = -1$ exactly satisfies recurrence.

patterns.title: Design a Divide-and-Conquer Algorithm
patterns.oneLiner: Split, recurse, combine cleverly. Usually aiming for $\Theta(n\log n)$ when brute force is $\Theta(n^2)$.
triggers: 'Design as efficient an algorithm as you can.'
Brute force is $O(n^2)$ and there's structure you can exploit.
'If you used divide and conquer, state a recurrence.'
Hint phrases like 'this can be done explicitly by D&C, or more simply by sorting'.
patterns.technique: Decide: (1) how to split (usually halve by some coordinate / attribute), (2) what each recursive call returns, (3) how to combine in linear (or $O(n\log n)$) time. Write the recurrence. Solve.
template: **Base case** for small sizes.
**Divide:** usually by median of some attribute; split array in half.
**Recurse:** call the algorithm on each half.
**Combine:** use the structure returned (sorted, extrema, etc.) to produce the answer for the whole in $O(n)$ if possible.
**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \text{combine}(n)$. Solve.
pitfalls: Combine step is $O(n \log n)$ by mistake (e.g. sorting inside recursion) → $\Theta(n \log^2 n)$ instead of $\Theta(n \log n)$. Precompute sorts outside recursion when possible.
Over-designing D&C when sort-then-scan is simpler and has the same asymptotic.
Forgetting that all points in $U_R$ automatically survive in Pareto-like problems (because their $x$ is larger than everything in $L$).

examples.why: Pareto-undominated points: sort-then-scan in $O(n \log n)$, or D&C with per-half combination.
3-way merge sort: $T(n) = 3T(n/3) + n = \Theta(n\log n)$. Still $\Theta(n\log n)$ — splitting into more pieces doesn't help.
Close-to-sorted: linear pass + sort tiny residual + merge = $O(n)$.

patterns.title: Design a Dynamic Programming Algorithm
patterns.oneLiner: Define subproblem, write recurrence, prove optimal substructure, fill order, runtime.
triggers: 'Determine whether this string can be segmented.'
'Maximum value obtainable.'
'Count the number of ways.'
'Longest/shortest sub[sequence|string].'
Naive recursion has overlapping subproblems.
patterns.technique: Follow the 5-step template (matches the exam grading rubric).
template: **Step 1: Subproblem.** Define $B[i]$ (or $DP[i,j]$) in WORDS. E.g. $B[i]$ = True iff $s[1..i]$ can be segmented.
**Step 2: Recurrence + base cases.** Express $B[i]$ in terms of strictly smaller subproblems. Include $B[0]$ or $B[0,*]$ bases.

**Step 3: Correctness.** Prove optimal substructure via exchange argument: assume OPT doesn't have claimed structure; swap out a suboptimal piece for a better one; derive a strictly better OPT' — contradiction.
**Step 4: Fill order.** Identify dependencies; fill table so each entry's dependencies come first. 1D prefix problems: left-to-right. 2D: row-by-row.
**Step 5: Runtime.** $(\text{#subproblems}) \times (\text{time per subproblem})$.
pitfalls: Vague subproblem definitions lead to broken recurrences. Always state the subproblem explicitly in words.
Forgetting base cases, especially $B[0]$.
Using top-down recursion when bottom-up iterative is clearer (professor prefers iterative).
Not proving the recurrence's correctness — especially the optimal-substructure part.

examples.why: Word break: $B[i] = \bigvee_j (B[i-j] \wedge s[i-j+1..i] \in D)$. $O(n^2)$ via $O(n)$ subproblems $\times$ $O(n)$ work each.

patterns.title: Binary-Search Variant on Sorted Input
patterns.oneLiner: Halve the search space. Might need extra bookkeeping (offset, monotone function construction).
triggers: Sorted array, need $O(\log n)$ or $O(\log m + \log n)$.
'Find an index where $X$ holds.'
Search in an unbounded / infinite space.
Median of two sorted arrays.
Row/column-sorted matrix (related — use staircase walk).
patterns.technique: Compare middle; eliminate half of the remaining range. Recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) = \Theta(\log n)$.
template: Identify a **monotone** quantity in the input (sorted values, $A[i] - i$, etc.).
Pick the middle index.
Decide which half the answer must lie in (use the monotonicity).
Recurse on that half.
For unbounded search: first double your range $1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots$ until you overshoot, then binary-search. Output-sensitive $O(\log k)$.
pitfalls: Non-distinct elements can break monotonicity tricks.
For $A[i] = i$: the function $f(i) = A[i] - i$ is non-decreasing because consecutive distinct integers differ by $\ge 1$.
For two-array median: shift target $k$ when you discard a lower half, don't when discarding an upper half.

examples.why: Find $A[i] = i$: $f(i) = A[i] - i$ is monotone; binary-search for zero crossing.
Row/col-sorted matrix: not pure binary search, but uses the same 'halve-the-possibilities' intuition — staircase walk from top-right.

patterns.title: Median of Medians with Non-Standard Group Size
patterns.oneLiner: Apply the pigeonhole formula: $(n/(2k)) \cdot \lceil k/2\rceil$ elements guaranteed on one side.
triggers: 'Split $n$ numbers into groups of $k$…'
'Let $M$ be the median of the group medians.'
'At most [fraction] of the original numbers could be greater than $M$.'
patterns.technique: Count: $n/k$ groups, half of them ($n/(2k)$) have group medians $\le M$. Each such group contributes $\lceil k/2\rceil$ elements $\le M$. Product: $(n/(2k))\cdot\lceil k/2\rceil$ guaranteed $\le M$.
template: Number of groups: $n/k$.
Group medians $\le M$: at least $n/(2k)$ (since $M$ is the median of group medians).
Each such group has $\lceil k/2\rceil$ elements $\le$ its median, hence $\le M$.
Guarantee: $\dfrac{n}{2k} \cdot \left\lceil\dfrac{k}{2}\right\rceil$ elements $\le M$.
Upper bound on elements $> M$: $n - $ (guarantee).
pitfalls: Using $k/2$ instead of $\lceil k/2\rceil$ for odd $k$.
Forgetting that the guarantee is one-sided (symmetric argument needed for $\ge M$).
$k = 3$ or smaller gives $a+b = 1$ or more in the Select recurrence — no longer linear. $k = 5$ is the smallest 'safe' group size.

examples.why: Group-of-9: $(n/18) \cdot 5 = 5n/18$ guaranteed $\le M$, so at most $13n/18$ can be $>M$. T/F claim #6.

patterns.title: Exchange Argument for Optimal Substructure
patterns.oneLiner: Assume OPT doesn't have the claimed structure; swap to build a strictly better solution; contradict optimality.
triggers: Proving correctness of a DP recurrence.
Showing 'any optimal solution must look like [something simpler]'.
Arguing optimal substructure in greedy problems.
patterns.technique: Consider an optimal solution OPT. Assume for contradiction it doesn't have the claimed structure (e.g. doesn't end in the right way). Surgically swap the offending piece for a better alternative. The modified ALT is still feasible and strictly better. Contradiction.
template: Let OPT be an optimal solution.
Suppose OPT doesn't have the claimed structure.
Identify the violating piece of OPT (e.g. a 'last step' that's suboptimal).
Show there's a better alternative for that piece (using the definition of subproblem optimality).
Construct ALT = OPT with the violating piece replaced. Verify ALT is still valid.
Compute value(ALT) > value(OPT). Contradiction.
pitfalls: Swapping something that breaks feasibility (make sure the replacement is valid in context).
Forgetting to handle ties (when value(ALT) = value(OPT)). Usually you can still argue OPT has the structure or use an averaging/choice argument.
Using the structure of the recurrence you're trying to prove — subtle circularity.

examples.why: Word break: any valid segmentation of $s[1..i]$ ends in some word of length $j$, and $s[1..i-j]$ must itself be segmentable. Otherwise, a better $s[1..i-j]$ segmentation would contradict $s[1..i]$ being segmentable — or ruin its segmentability.
